key_insight: A finite element skin model with viscoelasticity and anatomically inspired ligament attachments can reproduce contact-driven facial wrinkling with realistic temporal evolution.
engineering_relevance: This enables more physically grounded facial simulation for graphics, animation, and digital human systems where contact, compression, and soft-tissue detail matter.
method: Model skin as a viscoelastic layered material to capture rate-dependent wrinkle dynamics.
Use high-order prismatic solid-shell FEM elements to resolve through-thickness stress and fine deformation modes.
Introduce continuum-based skin ligaments to represent heterogeneous attachments and mobility constraints.
evidence_signal: The method was evaluated on synthetic examples and compared against real-world footage, showing temporally coherent and visually realistic wrinkle patterns during transient contact.
watch_item: High-order 3D solid-shell FEM with heterogeneous ligament modeling may be computationally expensive and sensitive to parameter calibration for subject-specific skin behavior.
paper_title: Physics-Based Simulation of Contact-Induced Facial Wrinkling
